title: What Is localhost?
subtitle: Understanding your computer as a server

sections.content:
You've been visiting `localhost:3000` in your browser, but what does that actually mean?

**localhost** is a special hostname that always means "this computer." Every computer knows that `localhost` refers to itself. Behind the scenes, it maps to the IP address **127.0.0.1** — a special address that loops right back to your own machine.

Think of it like calling your own phone number. The call doesn't go anywhere — it comes right back to you.

The **:3000** part is the **port number**. A single computer can run many servers at once, and the port number tells the browser *which one* to talk to.
sections.analogy: If your computer's IP address is like a building's street address, then port numbers are like apartment numbers. The mail carrier (your browser) needs both to deliver the request to the right place. Port 3000 is apartment 3000 in the building called localhost.
keyPoints: Port numbers distinguish between different servers on the same machine
Only YOU can access localhost — nobody else on the internet can see it

sections.question: What does localhost:3000 mean?
sections.explanation: localhost always means "this computer." The :3000 specifies port 3000. So localhost:3000 means: talk to the server running on port 3000 on my own machine.

sections.content:
Here's something important to understand: **your server is private right now.** Only your browser, running on your computer, can reach it. If you sent the URL `http://localhost:3000` to a friend, it wouldn't work for them — their browser would look for a server on *their* computer at port 3000, and they don't have one running.

This is actually a good thing. It means you can build, experiment, and break things without anyone else seeing your work. Your development server is your private workshop.

In **Level 7**, you'll learn how to deploy your server to the internet so anyone in the world can visit it. But for now, localhost is your safe space to build and learn.
sections.tip: You might see some tutorials use 127.0.0.1:3000 instead of localhost:3000. They mean the same thing. localhost is just the friendly name for 127.0.0.1.

completionMessage: Now you understand the address you've been typing. localhost is your own computer, and the port number tells it which server to connect to. Simple but powerful.
